retrieved_docs.title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Likewise, ocular muscle weakness, involuntary eye movement(nystagmus), protruded eyes, muscle fasciculations, and parkinsonism are typical for SCA1, 2, and 3. Pyramidal signs such as spasticity, muscle weakness, hyperreflexia are overlapped with most of the subtypes. SCA10 is more prevalent with epileptic seizures, SCA14 with jerky contractions of muscles (myoclonus), brief contractions of face and tongue (fasciculations) with SCA36, abnormal involuntary movement (chorea) and tremor in SCA12, 27, and ichthyosiform plaques with SCA 34. Abnormal movements or postures, cognitive and psychiatric problems, tremor, quick involuntary movements (chorea), hearing loss are other common findings. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Von Economo Encephalitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Parkinsonism with extrapyramidal signs due to basal ganglia involvement can be seen as an early or late complication. Patients will present stiffness and bradykinesia involving the upper extremities more than the lower. They can also show chorea, myoclonus, torsion spasms, and facial tics, including the tongue and palate. [1] Von Economo encephalitis can present with high fever, headache, double vision, vertigo, vomiting, catatonia, confusion, and increased tone in all four extremities. Catatonia presents with immobility, staring, negativism, stupor, and echolalia. Characteristic symptoms of hypersomnolence, easy arousable, ophthalmoplegia, and extrapyramidal signs following a recent upper respiratory tract infection suggest the development of autoimmune encephalitis. [3] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Millard-Gubler Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Focusing specifically on the brainstem, the patient should be assessed for any localizing symptoms: visual acuity and clarity, presence of diplopia, new visual impairment (eg, blind spots), trouble chewing, facial asymmetry, hearing changes, vertigo, incoordination or tremor, trouble swallowing, slurred speech, alterations in tongue mobility, tremor, and changes in gait or balance. The history of any headaches proximal to presentation should be explored.

retrieved_docs.title: Millard-Gubler Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Raymond-Cestan syndrome is caused by a lesion involving the upper dorsal pons. This syndrome manifests as ipsilateral ataxia and coarse intention tremor, ipsilateral paralysis of muscles of mastication and sensory loss in the face, contralateral sensory loss in the body, and contralateral hemiparesis of the face and the body. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Essential Tremor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Physiologic tremor: Predominantly bilateral, symmetrical action tremor. High frequency (10 to 12 Hz), the presence of known cause (e.g., medications, hyperthyroidism, hypoglycemia)

retrieved_docs.title: Intention Tremor -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Here are some important points to take into consideration:

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians should examine all patients for any movement disorders before initiating treatment with dopamine D2 receptor antagonists. Tardive dyskinesia presents clinically as stereotypical involuntary movements of the tongue, neck, and facial muscles, truncal musculature, and limbs. Buccolingual movements, including masticatory muscles, are characterized by lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, perioral movements, chewing movements, or a puffing of cheeks. Sometimes, these movements may be hard to distinguish from stereotype posturing seen in chronic psychotic patients. However, tardive dyskinesia is seen in patients with chronic exposure to dopamine D2 receptor blockade.

retrieved_docs.title: Intention Tremor -- History and Physical -- Physical Exam
retrieved_docs.content: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin tests can be useful to evaluate for end-point intention tremors, especially when the patient is asked to do the maneuver quickly. Fine finger movements can be used as well to assess coordination and speed in tasks such as finger or foot tapping, buttoning/unbuttoning shirt, grabbing an object such as a cup or pencil.  The tremor will increase when the extremity is approaching the target. Another characteristic of intention tremors is that the oscillating amplitude can be decreased when the eyes are closed. Rapid alternating movement maneuvers can be used to identify dysdiadochokinesia. Proprioception of the great toes can be impaired as well. Gait testing may reveal wide-based ataxia, with difficulties with tandem gait and a positive Romberg. The patient may have slow saccadic movements and nystagmus. Depending on the etiology, patients may have increased reflexes and extensor Babinski reflexes. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Intention Tremor -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Feedback and feedforward centers are impaired, leading to deficits in fine motor movement, coordination, precision, and poor communication between tracts affecting the somatosensory, motor, and cerebellar function.

retrieved_docs.title: Intention Tremor -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: It is associated with damage to the superior cerebellar peduncle, dentate nucleus, and surrounding cerebellar tracts in the brainstem and the thalamus.

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Essential Blepharospasm -- Differential Diagnosis -- Tardive Dyskinesia
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia presents with repetitive, involuntary movements around the orofacial region. There may be smacking of the lips, protraction of the tongue, or excessive blinking of the eyes. These movements tend to be irregular and asynchronous and involve muscles that are not innervated by the facial nerve, such as the masseter muscle or the external muscles of the tongue. Tardive dyskinesia is a potential complication of long-term neuroleptic use. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Essential Tremor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Essential tremor most often affects the hands and arms bilaterally and is symmetric, but cases of asymmetric essential tremor have also been reported. In cases of asymmetric essential tremor, the tremor was more severe in the non-dominant arm. It can also affect the head and voice and, uncommonly, the face, legs, and trunk. It varies from a low amplitude, high-frequency postural tremor of the hands to a much larger amplitude, a tremor that is activated by particular postures and actions. In most cases, the tremor frequency of essential tremor is 6 to 12 Hz. essential tremor becomes apparent in the arms when they are held outstretched; it typically increases at the end of goal-directed movements such as drinking from a glass or finger-to-nose testing. Amplitude tends to increase with age, while frequency tends to decrease with age. Although there are large variations in tremor amplitude and disability among patients with essential tremor, it is a disabling condition for a substantial proportion of affected individuals. A number of reports suggest that functional disability in essential tremor is associated with the amplitude of kinetic tremor in the upper limbs. Some patients with essential tremor develop enhanced physiologic tremor due to anxiety or other adrenergic mechanisms, thereby aggravating the underlying tremor. On a physical exam, essential tremor can be elicited during examination under 2 circumstances: with the arms suspended against gravity in a fixed posture and during goal-directed activity. Essential tremor is usually relieved by small amounts of alcohol (60% to 70%) but, in contrast with physiologic tremor, is not usually aggravated by caffeine. In some cases, additional cerebellar signs can be found, like abnormal tandem walking and mild ataxia.

retrieved_docs.title: Intention Tremor -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Intention tremor is defined as a rhythmic, oscillatory, and high amplitude tremor during a directed and purposeful motor movement, worsening before reaching the endpoint.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebellar Dysfunction -- Clinical Significance -- Signs of Cerebellar Vermis and Flocculonodular Lobe Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Gait Ataxia: Abnormal coordination of movements while walking. Patients have wide-based, drunk like unsteady, stumbling gait, which is also called “staggering gait.” The patient will be unable to walk on toes or heels and in tandem; also known as truncal ataxia. Titubation: Tremor (noodling) of the head or axial body. If severe, the patient can’t sit or stand without help. Nystagmus: Commonly bilateral, these are involuntary, rapid, repetitive eye movements, which can be horizontal or vertical.

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia manifests as involuntary choreoathetoid movements affecting orofacial and tongue muscles and, less commonly, the trunk and extremities. Although symptoms are typically not painful, they may impede social interaction and cause difficulty in chewing, swallowing, and talking. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Holmes Tremor -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: It is an extremely rare condition, with only 155 patients reported between 1904-2016.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2688
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors, indicated by the allowed qualifier, are recognized as relevant contributors to several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, a genetic component is associated with التسمم الوشيقي (viral intoxication), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperthyroidism), and قرحة قلاعية (aphthous ulcer). The presence of a genetic predisposition suggests that hereditary influences may play a role in the development or susceptibility to each of these disorders, underscoring the importance of considering family history and genetic screening in their assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2743
retrieved_docs.content: Chemically induced depression (اكتئاب), chemically induced intestinal viral infection (عدوى بفيروس معوي), chemically induced late movement disorder (خلل الحركة المتأخر), chemically induced incomplete spontaneous abortion (إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل), and chemically induced tongue disorder (اِضطِراب اللسان) are all medical conditions that can be qualified by a chemical etiology. Recognizing that these diverse disorders may arise as a result of chemical exposure or induction underscores the importance of evaluating chemical causes in their diagnosis, treatment planning, and overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is employed both for prevention and control as well as for therapeutic intervention across a range of clinical conditions. It is indicated for the prophylaxis and treatment of blood‑transfusion reactions (تفاعل نقل الدم), seizures (نوبة), tongue disturbances (اِضطِراب اللسان), paralysis (فالج), and lower‑extremity ulcers (قرحات الطرفين السفليين). In addition, the allowed qualifier is used to prevent, control, and treat headache, including migraine. Conversely, headache (including migraine) itself is a target for both preventive/controlling measures and therapeutic management. This integrated use of the allowed qualifier underscores its broad applicability in both safeguarding patients from the onset of these disorders and managing established disease states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1679
retrieved_docs.content: Chemically induced is identified as a qualifier that may be applied to several medical conditions. It is an allowed qualifier for عدوى بفيروس معوي (intestinal viral infection) and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). In addition, the qualifier can be used to describe خلل الحركة المتأخر (delayed movement disorder) and فطار غزوي (aggressive breakfast). Thus, chemically induced serves as a permissible descriptive term for these four entities, linking the concept of chemical induction to each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2961
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Allowed qualifier” is linked by the relationship “komplikace” to two distinct conditions: “انسداد الحالب” (ureteral obstruction) and “اِضطِراب اللسان” (tongue disorder). In this context, “Allowed qualifier” is identified as a complication of both ureteral obstruction and tongue disorder, highlighting the need for clinicians to consider this qualifier when evaluating patients with either of these conditions. Recognizing such complications can inform diagnostic assessment and guide appropriate management strategies for affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are linked to خلل الحركة المتأخر, a late‑onset movement disorder (commonly known as tardive dyskinesia) that can develop after prolonged use of these medications; recognizing this relationship is essential for clinicians to monitor patients on antipsychotic therapy for abnormal involuntary movements and to adjust treatment strategies to reduce the risk of this adverse effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation (إِمْساك) is clinically associated with vomiting (تَقَيُّء), indicating that patients who experience difficulty passing stool may also present with episodes of emesis, reflecting a possible shared gastrointestinal dysfunction or underlying condition that links these two symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: interrelated entities demonstrate how drug‑induced, medically‑caused, neurodegenerative, and age‑related psychiatric conditions intersect through shared diagnostic categories, overlapping symptom clusters (anxiety, mood disturbance, psychosis, confusional states, motor stereotypies, affective blunting), and common neurobiological mechanisms such as brain atrophy and cholinergic dysfunction, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2699
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is applicable to both diagnostic imaging and the complications of التهاب وريدي (venous inflammation), انسداد الحالب (ureteral obstruction), تفاعل نقل الدم (blood transfusion reaction), and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). It can be used to denote imaging findings for each of these conditions as well as to describe any complications that may arise from them.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: thrombotic disorders, motor deficits, meningeal injuries, migraine subtypes, and movement‑based assessments intersect to inform the diagnosis, classification, and management of paralysis, secondary parkinsonism, spinal cord injury, and other brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: The interpretation of “pohyb,” which means movement, is identified as “باركنسونية ثانوية,” or secondary parkinsonism, indicating that abnormal movement patterns are understood in the context of a secondary parkinsonian syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked through the relationship “enzymologie” to three specific clinical conditions: اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فالج (paralysis), and قرحات الطرفين السفليين (ulcers of the lower limbs). This indicates that enzymological considerations are recognized as permissible qualifiers when evaluating these disorders, suggesting that enzyme‑related mechanisms or assessments may be relevant in the diagnosis, pathophysiology, or management of tongue disturbances, paralysis, and lower‑extremity ulcerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory is described as a temporary medical condition whose clinical course is characterized by غشي, indicating that episodes of drowsiness or altered consciousness are a typical feature of its progression. This relationship underscores that patients experiencing a transitory condition may present with غشي as part of the condition’s natural course.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: aggressive behavior, and somatic symptoms like nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite, all organized within a common residual‑code framework that captures unstandardized or less‑specific medical entities for comprehensive coding, diagnosis, and patient care.
